Week of 2/16 - 2/20

Weekly Market Summary

By Noah Halfon

Global Weekly Market Movements

U.S. Equities

  • S&P 500 Index: 6,909.51 (1.10%)
    • SPX closed the week green after markets reacted positively to tariff rulings and manufacturing data. Leading the index was XLE energy, up 2.22%. XLI industrials followed closely at 2.08% while XLP consumer staples remained the only red of the week at -1.73% led by Walmart's forward looking guidance.
  • NASDAQ Composite: 22,886.07 (0.90%)
  • Dow Jones Industrial Average: 49,625.97 (0.38%)
  • Russell 3000 Index: 3,934.81 (1.27%)

Big Movers of the Week

    • Texas Pacific Land Corporation (TPL): 499.88 (21.42%)
      • Shares of the Texas landowner that holds oil and gas royalty saw shares rise after reporting strong Q4 earnings and growth tailwinds. EPS came in at $1.79, topping analyst estimates of $1.73, while revenue beat the $204 million consensus at $212 million. Royalty production also increased more than 20% YoY. TPL also committed $50 million to a partnership with AI datacenter Bolt Data & Energy and increased dividends 12.5% to $0.60 a share.
    • Moderna Inc (MRNA): 49.87 (19.68%)
      • Shares of MRNA rebound from last week as the FDA announces they will review the companies application for mRNA-1010 flu vaccine. Last week the FDA refused to review the regulatory filing needed to administer and sell dosage. If approved, MRNA plans to launch commercially for 2026 and 2027 vaccination seasons.
    • General Parts Co (GPC): 118.06 (-20.13%)
      • GPC falls after weak quarterly earnings and lower 2026 estimates. EPS missed estimates of $1.79 at $1.55 per share, while 2026FY EPS guidance dropped from $8.41 to around $7.50-$8.00 per share. GPC also has plans to split its business in 2027 with one part comprising its Automotive and the other Industrial parts groups. Although earnings showed that their international automotive segment sales rose due to favorable FX changes, analysts lowered price targets from highs of 175 to mid 130s.

Chinese Equities - Shanghai Composite (SHCOMP): 4,082.07 (-0.52%)

Hong Kong Equities - Hang Seng Index (HSI): 26,413.35 (-3.06%)

Japanese Equities- Nikkei 225 (NI225):

European Equities

  • FTSE 100 Index (UKX): 10,686.89 (2.30%)
  • German Index (DAX): 25,260.69 (1.09%)

Commodities

  • Gold Futures: 5,188.60 (3.85%)
  • Crude Oil Futures: 66.03 (5.48%)
    • Tensions in Iran escalate as Trump set a 10-15 day deadline for a nuclear deal. The US military has begun building a presence in the Persian Gulf while Iran has partially closed the Strait of Hormuz for live drills and announced joint naval exercises with Russia.

Crypto

  • Bitcoin (BTC): 67,574.57 (1.70%)
  • Ethereum (ETH): 1,951.82 (-0.11%)

U.S. Monetary & Fiscal Policy

Tariffs: In a 6-3 ruling the Supreme Court has overruled Trump’s Liberation Day tariffs. Claiming that Trump overstepped his authority in April when he invoked the International Emergency Economic Powers Act to justify levies across the globe. In response to his first real defeat in court, President Trump has responded by using Sections 122, 232, and 301 of multiple trade acts to impose a new 15% tariff on every country the US trades with for around the next 5 months. The US will now owe upwards of $130 billion back to companies, where the collection methods and amounts are still uncertain. The Supreme Court did not address the eligibility for refunds.

Global Macroeconomic News

PCE & Core PCE: December PCE comes in hot at 2.9% YoY vs the 2.8% expected, while Core PCE comes in at 3.0% YoY vs the 2.9% expected. Month over month both regular and core PCE rose 0.4% vs the 0.3% expected. This is the highest PCE reading we have gotten since November of 2023 as data shows good prices increasing. PCE puts more weight on healthcare costs and does not show shelter data that has recently been slowing. Rate cut odds for March dropped to under 4% as inflation concerns reignite.

Jobless Claims: Initial jobless claims come in lower than expected at 206,000 vs the 225,000 estimates. This is the lowest reading since November 2025, highlighting that the labor market is staying resilient despite Q4 GDP slowing and inflation worries coming back. Markets are now firmly pricing out a spring rate reduction, while we stay in a low hire low fire economy.

US GDP & Trade Deficit: Q4 GDP shows that the US economy grew just 1.4% in 2025, well below the 2.5% consensus and down sharply from Q3’s 4.4%. Much of this lag came from the 43 day government shutdown, where Federal spending fell 16.6% YoY cutting more than 1 percentage point from growth. America’s trade deficit gap also grew in December to $70.3 billion, with the US Commerce Department reporting that the shortfall culminated in a full-year deficit of $901.5 billion, one of the largest since 1960. Rising imports coupled with declining exports may indicate shifts in market dynamics that investors should watch.

Sources: Google Finance, Market Watch, CME Fedwatch, Yahoo Finance, Reuters, New York Times, Bloomberg, Wall St Journal, Washington Post, US Department of the Treasury, GuruFocus, Edward JonesNoah Halfon
